questions.question: What makes first-generation repeaters 'probabilistic'?

options.text: They use random numbers for encryption
Entanglement generation and swapping have probabilistic success
The output state is random
They may or may not be built

questions.explanation: First-generation repeaters are probabilistic because each operation (generation, swapping) has a success probability less than 1. Heralding signals indicate success, allowing the protocol to retry failed operations.

questions.question: In the DLCZ protocol, how is entanglement heralded?

options.text: By measuring both atoms
By detecting a photon after beam splitter interference
By classical communication between nodes
By checking memory coherence

questions.explanation: In DLCZ, both ensembles emit photons that interfere at a beam splitter. Detection of exactly one photon (which-path erased) heralds successful entanglement between the atomic ensembles without revealing which one contains the excitation.

questions.question: Why do first-generation repeaters need quantum memories?

options.text: To amplify quantum signals
To store successful segments while waiting for others
To perform classical computation
To generate random numbers

questions.explanation: Quantum memories store entanglement from successfully generated segments while the protocol waits for other segments. This enables parallel attempts and avoids requiring all segments to succeed simultaneously.

questions.question: What is the main challenge with nested swapping in first-gen repeaters?

options.text: It requires too much classical communication
Fidelity degrades multiplicatively through each swap
It is too fast for current technology
It creates too much entanglement

questions.explanation: Each swap multiplies fidelities: F_out ≈ F1 × F2. With N-1 swaps, final fidelity is approximately F^N (for identical F). This degradation is why purification is often needed between or after swapping.

questions.question: How does memory coherence time affect repeater performance?

options.text: It determines the maximum number of segments
It limits how long the repeater can wait for all segments
It only affects the output fidelity, not rate
It has no effect on first-generation repeaters

questions.explanation: If memory coherence time is shorter than the time needed to generate all segments, early segments expire before swapping. This creates a fundamental limit on the distance and number of segments that can be used.

questions.question: What is the typical BSM success rate with linear optics?
questions.explanation: Linear optics BSM can only distinguish 2 of 4 Bell states, giving maximum 50% success rate. This affects both swapping efficiency and overall repeater rate. Complete BSM requires nonlinear optics or auxiliary resources.

questions.question: First-generation repeaters have been demonstrated in laboratory settings.
questions.explanation: True. Several first-generation repeater demonstrations exist, including atomic ensemble experiments and NV center implementations. Distances of 10s of km have been achieved, with ongoing work toward practical deployment.
